name: Astarte Extended
description: Astarte, in her extended form, is a prominent goddess in Phoenician mythology, embodying fertility, sexuality, and war. Originating from earlier Mesopotamian deities like Ishtar, Astarte's influence spread throughout the ancient Near East and Mediterranean. She is often depicted as a powerful and beautiful woman, sometimes winged, symbolizing her dominion over the heavens and earth. Her powers extend to ensuring plentiful harvests, promoting love and passion, and granting victory in battle. Astarte's role is multifaceted; she is a protector of her people, a source of life and abundance, and a fierce warrior when provoked. She is associated with the planet Venus, representing both its morning and evening aspects. Temples dedicated to her were centers of worship where offerings were made to secure her blessings. Some interpretations link her to the sea, further expanding her domain. Sacrifices, both animal and potentially human in some contexts, were offered to appease her and ensure her continued favor. Astarte's worship often involved ritualistic practices aimed at stimulating fertility and celebrating the cycles of life. Her extended influence is evidenced by her adoption and adaptation in various cultures, reflecting her enduring appeal and the fundamental human concerns she represents. She is a powerful figure, representing the vital forces of nature and the complexities of human existence.
subtitle: Goddess of Fertility, Love, and War

keyMyths.title: Astarte and Baal
keyMyths.description: Astarte's relationship with Baal is central to Phoenician mythology, often involving conflicts and alliances. She supports Baal in his struggles against other deities.

keyMyths.title: Astarte and the Sea
keyMyths.description: Some myths portray Astarte as having dominion over the sea, reflecting the Phoenician reliance on maritime trade. She protects sailors and ensures safe voyages.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations of Phoenician art and inscriptions

keyMyths.title: Astarte's Temple
keyMyths.description: The establishment of temples dedicated to Astarte signifies her importance in Phoenician society. Offerings were made to ensure prosperity and divine favor.
